text: CHENAS, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Rhône, cant. of Beaujeu, 10 m. S of Mâcon. Pop. 700. It produces good wine.
CHENAY, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Deux-Sevres, cant. of Lezag, 13 m. NE of Melle. Pop. 1,215. It has a tile-work.
CHENAY-LE-CHATEL, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Saône-et-Loire, cant. of Marcigny. Pop. 1,078. CHE
CHEN-CHU, a district and town of China, in the prov. of Kwang-tung, division of Keun-che-fu, in N lat. 19° 32′ 24", and long. W of Pekin, 7° 29′ 20".
CHENDARI. See CHANDERI.
CHENDY. See SHENDY.
CHENDWASSA, a town of India, in Malwah, 18 m. S of Rampura, in N lat. 24° 13'.
CHENE (ISLE DU), a small island of Upper Canada, in Lake Ontario, off the E coast of Marysburgh township, and near the mouth of the Black river.

CHENE (LE), or LE CHESNE, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Ardennes, arrond. of Vouziers. The cant. comprises 18 com. The com., 10 m. N of Vouziers, had a pop. of 1,578 in 1841. There are iron forges here.
CHENE-THONEX, a parish and village of Switzerland, 3 m. E of Geneva, opposite Chene-les-Bougeries, on the Seime, an affluent of the Arve. Pop. 1,417. Large cattle fairs are held here.
CHENECEY, a commune of France, in the dep. of Doubs, cant. of Quingey, 8 m. SSW of Besançon, on the r. bank of the Loire. Pop. 1,013.
CHENE'E, a commune and village of Belgium, in the prov. and 8 m. SW of Liege, at the confluence of the Ourte and Vesdre. Pop. 1,688. There are extensive iron forges here.
CHENEHUTTE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Maine-et-Loire, cant. of Gennes. Pop. 1,106.
CHENELETTE, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Rhone, cant. of Saint-Nizier-d'Azergues, 18 m. NW of Villefranche. Pop. 685.
CHENERAILLES, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Creuse, arrond. of Aubusson. The cant. comprises 11 com. The com. and t wn are 11 m. N of Aubusson. Pop. 1,028.
CHENEVREY, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Haute-Saone, cant. of Marnay. Pop. 463.
CHEN-EU-TSUY, a village of China, in the prov. of Kiang-Nan, on the l. bank of the Yang-tse-kiang, 30 m. above Nanking, at the mouth of a canal leading to the neighbouring city of Ho-chow.
CHENEY, a commune of France, in the dep. of Yonne, cant. of Tonnere. Pop. 318.
CHENIERS, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Creuse, cant. of Bonnat, 15 m. N of Guret. Pop. 1,717.
CHENIMENIL, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Vosges, cant. of Bruyères. Pop. 1,082.
CHENIT (LE), a parish and village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Vaud, 20 m. WNW of Lausanne, at the entrance of the Orbe into the lake of Joux. Pop. 2,715, Protestants.

CHENOOKS, or CHINUKS, an Indian tribe, who inhabit the N side of the Columbia river, from its mouth to the Dalles, and are also found on Vancouver's island. Their numbers were very considerable at the beginning of the present century, but war and pestilence have reduced them to about 2,000. They are short and square framed, with broad faces, flat noses, and eyes turned obliquely upward, at the outer corner, like the Mongol race.
CHENKOURSK, a town of Russia, in the gov. of Archangel, on the r. bank of the Vosges, 200 m. SSE of Archangel. Pop. 500.
CHENONCEAUX, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Indre-et-Loire, cant. of Bléré, 6 m. S of Amboise, on the Cher. There is a magnificent chateau here built by Francis I.
CHENU, a commune of France, in the dep. of Sarthe, cant. of Le Lude. Pop. 1,145.
CHENOVE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Cote-d'Or, cant. of Dijon. Pop. 771.
CHEN-SI. See SHEN-SI.
CHENZENEK, a village of Persia, in the district of Ardabil, on an affluent of the Kora-su. Wheat and barley are cultivated here.
CHEPAK, a village of Persian Armenia, on the skirts of the plain, and 7 hours ENE of Kars.
CHEPELLO, an island in the bay of Panama, in N lat. 9°, W long. 79° 5′, 18 m. E of Panama.
CHEPEN, a town of Peru, in the dep. of Truxillo, near the mouth of the Zequetepec river.

CHEPEWYAN (Fort), a station in the Hudson's Bay company territories, on the Lake of the Hills, in N lat. 58° 40', W long. 110° 30'. Mackenzie embarked at this point, on 3d June 1789, and proceeded by the Slave lake and the river, since called Mackenzie's river, to the Arctic sea, in N lat. 69°, W long. 135°, whence he returned by the same route after an absence of 102 days.
CHEPICA, a small town of Chili, in the prov. of Coquimbo, near the coast, in S lat. 28°.
CHEPO, a town of China, in the prov. of Kweichu, division of Tu-yun-fu.
CHEPO, a town of New Granada, in the dep. of Ystmo, on a river of the same name, 40 m. NE of Panama. See Ballano.

CHEPSTOW, a parish, port, and market-town, in the county of Monmouth; 15 m. E by N of Newport; and 135 m. W of London; pleasantly situated on the side of a hill, on the W bank of the Wye, near its confluence with the Severn. Area of p. 1,020 acres. Pop. in 1801, 2,080; in 1831, 3,524; in 1841, 3,366. The streets of the town are neatly built and lighted with gas. The church, formerly the conventual church of a priory of Benedictine or Black monks, and founded as early as the reign of King Stephen, has been recently restored to its former dimensions, by the rebuilding of its chancel and transepts. The W doorway is richly decorated; the interior, with its grand range of semicircular arches reposing on massive piers, has an imposing effect. The advantageous situation of C., near the mouth of the Wye, is supposed to have rendered it a desirable position both in Roman and Saxon times; hence its encampments, and its castle, and fortifications. The Wye is navigable for large vessels only to C. bridge, but barges from 18 to 30 tons burthen can go as high as Hereford. The tide runs up the river with wonderful rapidity, frequently rising above 50 ft.; it has even been known to rise to 70 ft. "From the form of the British channel," says De La Beche, "and the absence of a free passage for the waters, such as exists at the straits of Dover in the English channel, westerly winds force up and sustain a great body of water, thereby raising the sea above the mean level several feet." There is a well here which ebbs and flows inversely with the flow and ebb of the tide. When the tide is at its height it is perfectly dry; a little before which it begins to subside, and soon after the ebb it returns. Neither wet nor dry weather affects it; but its increase and decrease regularly correspond with the tide. The well is only 32 ft. deep, and has frequently 14 ft. water, which is remarkably good. Ship-building is carried on to a considerable extent in the docks on the banks of the Wye. In these, vessels of 600 tons are built for the Baltic trade. Great quantities of salmon caught in the Severn, and the Wye fisheries, are sent from C. to London and other places. There are no manufactures carried on in C., but its foreign trade is considerable. Wines are imported from Oporto; and deals, hemp, flax, and pitch, from Norway and Russia. It is the port of all the towns on the rivers Wye and Lug; and supplies Herefordshire and the E part of Monmouthshire with all the necessary imports. C. exports bark, iron, cider, coal, and millstones; it sends a vast quantity of timber to Plymouth, Portsmouth, and Deptford; and the Bristol market depends upon it in a great measure for its supply of grain.-Numerous ancient encampments are traceable in the vicinity.-C. castle, or Castle Gwent, stands in a bold and commanding position, on a rock CHE of considerable elevation, of which it occupies the entire summit. It is constructed so near the river that it appears to form part of the perpendicular cliff on which it stands. The river flows close to its walls. The magnificent remains of this immense pile form a striking and picturesque object from almost every point in which it can be viewed. Towards the land side it was defended by a vast moat, and the walls were flanked with lofty bastion towers.-Over the Wye at C. there was at one time a curious old wooden bridge of great height, erected on piles, which has been displaced by an elegant iron one, erected at the joint expense of the counties of Gloucester and Monmouth; the river Wye forming the line of separation between them. The passage over the Severn, within 2 m. of the town, has also been greatly improved by the erection of stone-piers and the establishment of steam-packets.
CHEPUNSKI-NOSS, a headland on the coast of Kamschatka, which, with Cape Gavarea, forms the deep bay in which the harbour of Petrapaulski is situated.

CHEPUTNECTICOOK, a river of New Brunswick, descending from a ridge of mountains of primary formation and uniting with the St. Croix, 40 m. from its mouth, just above its great fall. It is broken by numerous rapids and falls, and expands in the upper part of its course into a series of lakes extending about 45 m. in length, and varying in breadth from a <math>\frac{1}{4}</math> m. to 10 m.

CHEPZA, a small river of Russia, in the gov. of Viatka, which, rising to the NE of Debyosin, runs NW along the foot of a ridge of hills, passes a small village to which it gives name, 41 versts E of Suri, and falls into the Viatka.

CHER, a river of France, which rises at the hamlet of Cher, near Merinchal, in the dep. of Creuse; flows N into the dep. of Allier, in which it passes Montluçon; enters the dep. of Cher, on its S frontier, and flows through it in a NNW course, passing St. Amand, Chateauneuf, and Vierzon, and receiving the Marmande and the Auron on the r., and the Theols on the l. At Thenioux, it enters the dep. of Loir-et-Cher, and flowing W, passes Chatres, Selles, and St. Agnau; receives the Saudre on the r., and leaves the dep. on its SW frontier, near St. Georges, entering that of Indre-et-Loire, in which it passes Blere, and falls into the Loire below Tours by two mouths, forming with that river a large island. Its total course is about 220 m., of which about onethird is navigable. It was the Carus of the Romans. -There is another small river of the same name, in the dep. of Ille-et-Vilaine, which flows into the Vilaine, on the l. bank, near Langon, after a course of 35 m.

CHER, a central department of France, principally formed of the E part of the ancient prov. of Berry; bounded on the N by the dep. of Loiret; on the E by the Loire, which separates it from the dep. of Nievre; on the S by the dep. of Allier; and on the W by the dep. of Indre, and of Loir-et-Cher. Its outline is very irregular. Its greatest length from N to S is about 86 m.; its greatest breadth 56 m. Its superficies is estimated at 718,138 hectares. Its surface is generally level, but diversified by a few hilly ridges of moderate elevation. The general declination is from S to N. The Loire and the Cher are the principal rivers; all its large streams flow into the Cher. About 37,000 hectares of surface are covered with hills; and 94,000 are waste land. The cultivable surface amounts to 380,000 hect. The principal agricultural productions are grain, wine, lint, chestnuts, and fruits. In 1839, the live stock in this dep. consisted of 32,785 horses, 2,464 mules, 5,234 asses, 114,460 oxen, 829,438 sheep, 37,739 pigs, and 14,924 goats. The mineral productions are iron, | lithographic stones, marble, gypsum, ochre and porcelain clay. The produce of the iron mines in 1839 was 1,015,560 quintals, valued at 1,003,472 francs. The iron trade, and the branches of manufacture connected with it, afford employment to a large proportion of the population. Fine woollen cloths, porcelain, beetroot sugar, nut and colza oil, paper, and glass, are also manufactured. The principal articles of exportation are grain, wine, iron, wool, cattle, wood, and flax. Trade and commerce are here facilitated by good roads, and by the Canal du Berri, which has a course nearly parallel to the Cher, between Chambon and Ainey, and the lateral canal of the Loire from Digoin to Briare.-The dep. is divided into three arrondissements, viz.: Pop. in 1846. Pop. in 1841. These arrondissements are subdivided into 29 cantons, and 293 communes. The dep. forms, with that of Indre, the diocese of the archbishop of Bourges. It returned 4 deputies, who were elected in 1834 by 1,037 electors, under the old regime. It has a royal college at Bourges, and two communal colleges at Saint-Amand and Sancerre. In 1840 it possessed 240 elementary schools.—Its cap. is Bourges. Its towns, with a pop. exceeding 3,000, are: Saint- Amand, Chateaumeillant, Dun-le-Roi, Henrichemont, Mehun, Sancerre, Vierzon-Ville, and Vierzon- Village.
CHERAC, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Charente - Inferieure, cant. of Burie. Pop. 1,822.
CHERAF, a town of Turkey in Asia, in Anadolia, in the sanjak of Kojah-Ili, on the coast of the Black sea, in N lat. 41° 10'.
CHERANCE', a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Sarthe, cant. of Beaumont-sur-Sarthe. Pop. 1,100.

CHERASCO, a town in the Sardinian states, in the prov. of Mondovi, in Piedmont, near the confluence of the Stura and the Tanaro, 27 m. SE by S of Turin. It is finely built; and was at one time strongly fortified; but its citadel was demolished in 1796, after its capture by the French. Pop. 9,000.
CHERATTE, a commune and town of Belgium, in the prov. and 8 m. NE of Liege. Pop. 1,918. Coal is wrought here.

CHERAW, a town in Chesterfield district, in the state of South Carolina, U.S., on the W bank of Great Pedee river, 110 m. above Georgetown. Pop. 1,100. The river is navigable for steam-boats to this place.

CHERBOURG, an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of La Manche. The arrond. comprises the 5 cant. of Beaumont, C., Octeville, Saint-Pierre-Eglise, and Les Pieux. Area 59,851 hectares. Pop. in 1831, 75,488; in 1841, 79,654; in 1846, 83,329.—The cant. comprises the single commune of C. The commune and town of CHERBOURG, one of the most important seaports of France, is situated at the bottom of a large bay, between Cape La Hogue, at the W extremity of Lower Normandy, on the W, and the E point of the Rae-de-Gatteville; in N lat. 49° 38′ 34″, W long. 1° 37′ 39″; 185 m. WNW of Paris, and 59 m. SSW & W from the Needles, at the embouchure of the Divette, on the flat margin of beach below the Mont-du-Roule and the Roc-au- Chat, part of a ridge of hills which form a kind of amphitheatre on the land side. It is a compact, CHE solidly built town, with narrow, crooked streets; but has been greatly improved within the last twenty years, during which period the court-house, halle-auble, gaol, and many of the handsome houses facing the merchant-basin, have been erected. The public establishments are a navigation school, a theatre, a public garden, a library of 4,000 vols., the public baths, and the promenade de l'Abbaye. The pop. in 1789 was 10,790; in 1841, 23,408; in 1846, 23,013. Some woollen and cotton stuffs are manufactured, and small coasting-vessels are built here. The chief articles of trade are corn, fruit, eggs, and ships' provisions, the surrounding country being well-adapted both for tillage and pasturage; but C. is rather a port of consumption than of export. The exports in 1841 amounted to 76,948 quintals; while the imports amounted to 252,071 quintals. The tonnage of the port, in the same year, was 240 vessels = On the land side, C. presents nothing remarkable; but towards the sea it is a place of the greatest interest. It has long been considered one of the five ports militaires, or principal stations of the French marine; and its improvement—for naturally it is no harbour-has, from time to time, occupied the attention of government for a century and a half. A plan to this effect was first proposed by the celebrated Vauban, but not carried into execution. During the reign of Louis XV., however, the harbour was so far enlarged as to be made capable of containing 160 merchant-vessels of tolerable size; but it was destroyed by the English in 1758, along with the magazines. After the peace of 1783, the French government determined on making C. a great naval depot. The project was to form not a dock-yard or inner basin, but a great roadstead, on the plan of breaking the force of the waves by sinking vast quantities of stone in the water. This plan was however rejected after consideration; and the government decided on erecting in the water a number of large masses of a conical shape, standing at a certain distance from each other, so as to admit the sea, while they should break its violence by the intervening erections. Vast cones or frames of wood, having a base of 140 ft. diam., and 60 ft. in diam. at the summit, were constructed and sunk, after which they were filled by throwing in immense quantities of stones. This work was commenced in 1784; but it proved unable to resist the violence of the winter-tempests, and was at last totally thrown down, forming a sort of sloping mound of stone and gravel. The government now conceived the plan of consolidating the immense deposits thus made in the water, by covering the whole over with blocks of masonry. This was effected after 1803, but the mass of small stones proved an insecure foundation for the superincumbent blocks, and the winter-storms-particularly that of 11th February, 1808—overturned vast pieces of the masonry, and nearly annihilated the labours of 16 years. The work was again resumed, and again nearly destroyed by a storm in 1836; but it still advances. The complete design of the digue or breakwater is to form a line of from 3 to 4 m. in length, running nearly WNW from the Isle Pilee towards Querqueville. As yet only half this distance appears above water. The width of the dyke at its base is 295 ft. The depth of the sea about it is from 36 to 45 ft. Up to 1839, 29,000,000 francs had been expended on this great work. It was apparent that a fleet at anchor within this bulwark would be exposed to danger from fire-ships, and, in certain winds, to bombardment by a superior enemy. The spot fixed on was situated on the W shore of the roadstead, a short distance NW of the town, where the bay sweeping round abruptly to the NW forms a rocky point on which a formidable battery stands, commanding the W entrance and most of the anchoring-ground within the breakwater. The work was prosecuted with great vigour; and by 1813 there was dug a basin extending about 1,000 ft. in length, and 770 ft. in width, and occupying a surface of about 18 acres. This basin has a depth of 25 ft. at low water, and is capable of containing fully 30 sail-of-the-line. In 1833, four slips or stocks for first rate men-of-war were added to the equipments of this dock. They are 117 metres = 383 ft. in length, and 26 metres = 85 ft. in height. Bonaparte's next project was a wet dock. It was begun in 1813, and is now finished. The largest outer basin is 400 yards square, opening to the roads by a passage 100 yards wide. The inner basin is 360 yards long by 260 wide, cut out of the solid slate-rock, which is faced with granite, and communicating with the outer basin by flood-gates. These works have been greatly aided by inexhaustible quarries of granite and flint-stone within I m. of the town, on the Mont-du-Roule, whence a railroad carries the stone to the head of the merchant-basin. The opposite hill, called the Roc-au-Chat, is crowned by a telegraph communicating with one in the town below, and with Paris in 3 min. 56 sec.—The commercial port of C., to the E of the basin, is entirely separated from the government - docks. It is enclosed by a dike 1,933 toises in length; and is protected by three forts. The outer basin of the tidal-harbour is entered between two jetties which run out into the bay for about 300 yards. This harbour is nearly dry at low water; but it leads into a noble basin with solid granite quays and facings, and is capable of containing 100 sail of merchantmen. The sea is gaining on the land here.—C. is a place of great antiquity. It was the Casaris Burgus, Caroburgus, or Chereburgum, of the middle ages. In 1418 it was taken by the English, and in 1450 was retaken by Charles VII. In 1758 the public works of C. were destroyed by an English expedition under General Bligh, and a contribution of 44,000 livres levied on the town.
CHERBOROUGH. See SHERBOROUGH.
CHERCHAL. See SHERSHAL.
CHERCHER, a town and district of Abyssinia, in the Galla country, 6 days SE of Ankobar.
CHERDE-MODO-ALIN, a mountain of Inner Mongolia, in N lat. 40° 52′ 3″, and long. W of Pekin 4° 12′ 40″.

CHERE, a river of France, in the dep. of the Loire-Inferieure, which takes its rise to the E of Chateaubriand; runs W; and after a course of 39 m. falls into the Vilaine, on the confines of the dep. of the Ille-et-Vilaine.
CHE'RECHOV, a town of Russia in Europe, in the gov. and 80 m. SSE of Grodno, district and 9 m. ESE of Proujani.
CHE'RENCE'- LE - ROUSSEL, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Manche, cant. of Juvigny, 7 m. from Mortain. Pop. in 1841, 991. It contains numerous paper-mills.
CHE'RENG, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Nord, cant. of Lannoy. Pop. in 1846, 1,291.

CHERENTES, or XERENTES, a tribe of Indians in Brazil, who inhabit a district in the vicinity of the Tocantins river, between Preto and Maranhao. They are of warlike habits, but tractable and industrious.
CHERES (LES), a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Rhône, cant. of Limonest, 12 m. NW of Lyon. Pop. 593.
CHERGORA, a town of Turkey in Asia, in Ana- | rounded by solid ice in June and July. CHE dolia, in the sanjak and 15 m. WSW of Kastamuni.
CHERHILL, a parish of Wilts, 21 m. E of Calne. Area 2,370 acres. Pop. 422.
CHERI. See KIERI.

CHERIAGHAUT, or CHIRIYAGHAT [i. e. 'the Bird passage'], a strong pass in Nepal, between Bichnaker and Hethaura, 7 m. from the former place. It is commanded by two hills; but the road is good, even for loaded cattle. CHERIBON, SHERIBON, or TCHERIBON, a residency, or administrative district of Java, extending across the island from N to S, at the point where its breadth, by an abrupt indentation of both coasts, is suddenly reduced from 100 m. to 50 m. It comprises 1,334 sq. m. Its pop. was estimated in 1838 at 545,130. It is remarkably fertile in timber, coffee, indigo, sugar, and pepper. The subdivision called Găband yields rice of fine white grain equal to that of Carolina [Crawfurd]. A volcanic mountain, not far from the coast, to the SSW of the town of C., sometimes discharges smoke. It is called Dshirmai or Tjerimai, and is estimated to exceed 8,000 ft. in height.—The rhinoceros is seen on the hills and in the forests, within the limits of this district; and there is a kind of speckled deer which is kept tame in parks. The horses are good, small, and well-made, but vicious.—After the Dutch established themselves in Java, the princes of C. were obliged to deliver all the marketable produce of their country to the Dutch East India company, at certain fixed prices. These potentates seem to have lived in considerable splendour. They put themselves under the protection of the Dutch in 1680, and the criminal judicature of their territory was thereupon combined with that of the Dutch resident. When the British were in possession of Java, there were no fewer than four princes to whose ancestors the territory of C. belonged; and certain districts or pensions were allotted to them for their support.—The cap. of the district is a town on the N coast, of the same name, situated at the bottom of a deep bay, 178 m. E of Batavia. It was formerly a station of some importance to the Dutch, carrying on a considerable trade; and the European town, which contains many good houses, was well peopled, until within a few years of the arrival of the British in Java, in 1812, when a pestilential disease, ascribed to a neighbouring morass, swept off many of the inhabitants. It has since regained its activity, and has a pop. of about 10,000. It contains a fine mosque. There is a large Chinese village in the vicinity.

CHERICAL, a small district of Hindostan, forming a subdivision of the prov. of Malabar, in N lat. 12°. It was formerly an extensive territory. CHERIE, CHERRY, or BEAR ISLAND, a small uninhabited island of the Arctic ocean, between Norway and Spitzbergen, in N lat. 74° 33′, and E long. 17° 44' [Beechey], N lat. 74° 30' [Von Buch]. It is entirely composed of secondary rocks, horizontally stratified, and cut perpendicularly on the coast into cliffs. The rocks are principally sandstone and limestone, the latter abounding in sea-shells. The sandstone contains a bed of coal of from 2 to 4 ft. in thickness. Barentz, who landed upon it in 1596, gave it the name of Bear island; it was afterwards called Cherie island by Stephen Bennet, in 1603, after Sir Francis Cherie. Soon after its discovery the Muscovy company of England obtained their supplies of oil for some years from the walrusses which they captured on this island.
