CHERI-KINI, a town of Turkey in Europe, in Rumelia, on the Black sea, 20 m. N of Constantinople.
CHERIER, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Loire, cant. of Just-en-Chevelet. Pop. 1,195.
CHERINGTON, a parish of Gloucestershire, 4 m. NNE of Tetbury. Area 1,880 acres. Pop. 220.— Also a parish of Warwickshire, 31 m. SE of Shipton - upon - Stour, S of the Stour. Area 890 acres. Pop. 340.
CHE'RISY, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Eure-et-Loir, cant. and 3 m. ENE of Dreux, on the r. bank of the Eure. Pop. 1,072.

CHERITON, a parish of Glamorganshire, 14 m. WNW of Swansea, on the Bury. Pop. 283.—Also a parish of Southampton, 3 m. S of New Alresford. Area 2,980 acres. Pop. 556. - Also a parish of Kent, 23 m. WNW of Folkestone, on the South Eastern railway. Area 1,790 acres. Pop. 1,178.
CHERITON - BISHOP, a parish of Devon, 6 m. SW of Crediton. Area 5,150 acres. Pop. 848.
CHERITON-FITZPAINE, a parish of Devon, 5 m. NNE of Crediton, near a branch of the Crudy. Area 4,770 acres. Pop. 1,156.
CHERITON (NORTH), a parish of Devon, 3 m. SSW of Wincanton. Area 1,290 acres. Pop. 290.
CHERITON (South), a hamlet in the p. of Horsington, Devon. Pop. 414.

CHERKASK, a town of Russia, in the country of the Don Cossacks, 36 m. ENE of Azof, and 276 NW of Georgiefsk, on an island formed by the Don and the Aksai. The houses are chiefly of wood, and a large proportion are built on piles, at a height of from 4 to 6 ft. from the ground, to protect them from the inundations to which the locality is liable from the beginning of April to the end of June. Viewed from the river, during the period of inundation, C. affords a novel spectacle. The entrance to it is by broad canals, and on each side the houses, built upon piles, appear to float upon the water; while the inhabitants pass between them in boats, or by narrow bridges formed by a couple of planks. When Clarke visited this town in 1800, it contained about 3,000 houses, and 15,000 inhabitants, a mixture of various nations, principally Circassians, Malo- Russians, and Russians, but also of Tartars, Poles, Greeks, Turks, Calmucks, and Armenians. C. was the cap. of the territory until the removal of the seat of government to New C.

CHERKASK (New), or Novo-Tcherkesk, a town of Russia, the cap. of the country of the Don Cossacks, on the Aksai, a tributary of the Manytsch, in N lat. 47° 21', about 54 m. NE of the mouth of the Manytsch, 15 m. NNE of Old Cherkask, and 1,720 versts from St. Petersburg. Pop. 11,327. This town was founded in 1806, at the recommendation of the hetmann Platoff; but it appears to have made little progress. Its buildings are chiefly of wood. A wide plain stretches along both sides of the Manytsch from C. to the borders of the Caspian, and is inhabited by various Calmuck tribes. A line of Cossack posts are drawn from this town to Lake Manytsch, and thence in a N direction along the borders of the government of Astrakhan.
CHERMIANTSK, an extensive copper-work of Russia in Europe, in the gov. of Perm, district and 26 m. SE of Osa.
CHERMICOS, a town of Spain, in Andalusia, in the prov. of Almeria, partido and 11 m. SE of Purchena.

CHERMONTANE, a few huts or chalets at the head of the Val-de-Bagnes, in the cant. of Valais in Switzerland. A magnificent sea of ice, called the glacier of C., which is described by Bourrit and by Professor Forbes, fills the entire head of the valley. CHE 486
CHERNEIX, a village of France, in the dep. of the Ille-et-Vilaine, cant. and 5 m. NE of Dol. Pop. 1,509.
CHERNEX, a village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Vaud, district of Vevay, beautifully situated at the foot of the Kuble.

CHEROKEE, a county of the state of North Carolina, U. S., comprising an area of 1,000 sq. m., partly mountainous, but well-watered and very fertile. It contains marble of various colours, iron-ore, gold, and in small quantities silver and lead. Pop. in 1840, 3,427, of whom 3,205 were whites, 199 slaves, and 23 free-coloured.—Also a county of the state of Georgia, comprising an area of 620 sq. m., drained by the Etowah and its branches. It presents a hilly surface, but on the banks of the streams is extremely fertile. Pop. 5,895, of whom 5,401 were whites, 491 slaves, and 3 free-coloured. Cap., Canton.—Also a county in the state of Alabama, comprising an area of 600 sq. m. It is hilly, but near the streams very fertile. Pop. 8,773, of whom 7,652 were whites, 1,112 slaves, and 9 free-coloured.—Also a village of M'Arthur township, Logan co., in the state of Ohio, 75 m. NW of Columbus, consisting in 1840 of about 40 dwellings. CHEROKEES, -in their own language, TSULAKEES, TSALAKEES, or TSALAGIS,-a once numerous and powerful tribe of Indians, the mountaineers of aboriginal America. Their territories originally comprised the upper valley of the Tennessee river, as far W as Muscle shoals, and the highlands of Carolina, Georgia, and Alabama,—a tract containing more than 35,000,000 acres, and forming the most picturesque and salubrious region E of the Mississippi. "Their homes," says Bancroft, the historian of the United States, warmed into poetical feeling by the traditional histories of this race, "were encircled by blue hills, rising beyond hills, of which the lofty peaks would kindle with the early light, and the overshadowing ridges envelop the valleys like a mass of clouds. There the rocky cliffs, rising in naked grandeur, defy the lightning, and mock the loudest peals of the thunder-storm; there the gentler slopes are covered with magnolias and flowering foresttrees, decorated with roving climbers, and ring with the perpetual note of the whip-poor-will; there the wholesome water gushes profusely from the earth in transparent springs; snowwhite cascades glitter on the hill-sides; and the rivers, shallow, but pleasant to the eye, rush through the narrow vales, which the abundant strawberry crimsons, and coppices of rhododendron and flaming azalea adorn. At the fall of the leaf, the fruit of the hickory and the chestnut is thickly strown on the ground. The fertile soil teems with luxuriant herbage, on which the roebuck fattens; the vivifying breeze is laden with fragrance; and daybreak is ever welcomed by the shrill cries of the social nighthawk and the liquid carols of the mocking-bird. Through this lovely region were scattered the little villages of the Cherokees, nearly fifty in number, each consisting of but a few cabins, erected where the bend in the mountain stream offered at once a defence and a strip of alluvial soil for culture. Their towns were always by the side of some creek or river, and they loved their native land; above all, they loved its rivers—the Keowee, the Tugeloo, the Flint, and the beautiful branches of the Tennessee. Running waters, inviting to the bath, tempting the angler, alluring wild fowl, were necessary to their paradise. Their language, like that of the Iroquois, abounds in vowels, and is destitute of the labials. Its organization has a common character, but etymology has not yet been able to discover conclusive analogies between the roots of words. The 'beloved' people of the Cherokees were a nation by themselves. Who can say for how many centuries, safe in their undiscovered fastnesses, they had decked their war-chiefs with the feathers of the eagle's tail, and listened to the counsels of their 'old beloved men?' Who can tell how often the waves of barbarous migrations may have broken harmlessly against their cliffs, where nature was the strong ally of the defenders of their land?" Between the close of the revolutionary war and the year 1820, the C. sold to the United States, at different times, more than three-fourths of their possessions; and on the 23d of May, 1836. a treaty was made with them by which they agreed, for 5,000,000 dollars, to remove W of the Mississippi where the undisturbed possession of 7,800,000 acres of land, with the right of self-government, was guaranteed them for ever, that is until the interests of the white race require their further removal. In 1825, the number of C. of pure and mixed blood, E of the Mississippi, was 13,563; and 147 white men, and 73 white women, had intermarried with them and were residing among them. They also held 1,277 negro slaves. In 1832, their number was estimated at 15,060, but in a document laid before congress in 1844, it was stated at 26,911.—The C. have made considerable progress in agriculture, and even in literature and the arts of civilized life. As far back as 1824, they adopted written laws; and they were the first aboriginal natives W of the Mississippi that did so. A well-organized system of government, it is reported, has been established amongst them. The executive consists of a principal chief and assistant, with 3 executive counsellors, all elected by the legislative body. The legislature consists of two bodies, a national committee and a national council, the former containing 16 members, the latter 24. The members are chosen for the term of two years, by the qualified electors in their several districts. These electors include all free male citizens who have attained the age of 18 years, except persons of African origin. The rules respecting the nature and powers of the legislature in general, are similar to those of the several states in the Union. Each of the two bodies has a negative on the other, and together they are styled 'the general council of the C. nation.' The chief and his assistant hold their offices for four years; the executive counsellors are chosen annually. The judiciary consists of a supreme court, and of circuit and inferior courts. The members of the supreme court hold their offices for four years. They have also a public treasury, a printing-office, and a newspaper, the Cherokee Phanix, commenced in February, 1828, under the editorship of a Cherokee. The press is owned and directed by the C. government. They have founts of types in the C. character; and the Gospel of Matthew, the Acts of the Apostles, and a collection of hymns, translated by one of the missionaries, have been printed in this character. Intermarriages have in many instances taken place between the C. and the whites in their neighbourhood; and many of the half breeds have large plantations, and carry on agriculture with more spirit than the full-blooded C. There are very different degrees of improvement among the members of the tribe. Some families have risen to a level with the white population of the U.S., while the improvement of others has just commenced. In general, those of mixed blood are in advance of the full-blooded Indians. Not less than a quarter of the people are probably in a greater or less degree of mixed blood.—The dress of most of the C. is substantially the same as that of the whites around them. A great part of their clothing is manufactured by themselves, though not a little is of the fabrics of New England and foreign countries-calico, broadcloths, silk. Many families raise their own cotton, out of which the women make substantial cloth. Cultivation by the plough is almost universal: most families raise enough to supply their own wants, and many have considerable quantities of corn for sale. None of them depend, in any considerable degree, on game for a support. The C. live chiefly in villages, and their dwellings are mostly comfortable log cabins, with chimneys, and generally floored. Many of their houses are decent buildings of two stories, some are even handsome dwellings of painted wood or brick. Polygamy is becoming rare, and women are no longer treated as servants, but are allowed their proper place. Superstition is rapidly declining, and the ancient traditions are fading from memory, so that it is difficult to collect them. Conjuring, however, is still practised to a considerable extent. In regard to intemperance, the C. would not suffer by a comparison with the white population around them. The laws rigorously exclude intoxicating liquors from all public assemblies, and otherwise restrict their use. They have among them temperance societies on the principle of entire abstinence; and the civil officers enforce the laws against the introduction of ardent spirits, and fine transgressors.
CHERON (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Seine-et-Oise, cant. of Dourdan. Pop. 1,125.
CHERONNAC, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Haute-Vienne, cant. and 7 m. from Rouchechouart. Pop. 1,047. It contains extensive iron-works.

CHE'ROY, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Yonne, arrond. of Sens. The cant. comprises 18 com. Pop. in 1831, 8,318; in 1841, 8,666. The town stands on a height, near the 1. bank of the Lunain, 16 m. W of Sens. Pop. 886. It possesses an important trade in cattle and fowls, and has several annual fairs.
CHERRA, a village of Afghanistan, 186 m. NW of Kandahar, on the road to Herat.
CHERRE', a commune of France, in the dep. of the Sarthe, cant. of La Ferté-Bernard, 21 m. SE of Mamers. Pop. 1,504.
CHERREPE, a cape and port of Peru, in the intendancy and 85 m. NNW of Truxillo. The port is small and shallow, and but little frequented.
CHERRHONISI (CAPE), a promontory of the island of Negropont, on the E coast, 28 m. E of Negropont.
CHERRINGTON, a township in the p. of Edgmont, Salop, 51 m. E of Newport, and S of the Mees. Pop. 189.
CHERRINGTON, or CHURTON, a parish of Wilts, 4 m. NNE of West Lavington. Area 2,040 acres. Pop. 428. CHE
CHERRUEIX, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Ille-et-Vilaine, cant. of Dol, on the Bay of Cancale, 181 m. S of Saint-Malo. Pop. 1,917. In the vicinity is an extensive sole-fishery.
CHERRY, a township of Butler co., in the state of Pennsylvania, U. S. Pop. in 1840, 625.
CHERRY, an island of the South Pacific, in Queen Charlotte's group, in S lat. 11° 40', and E long. 169° 30'. It is about 1 m. in length, and is cultivated. It was discovered in 1791.
CHERRY-BURTON. See Burton-Cherry.

CHERRY-CREEK, a township of Chautauque co., in the state of New York, U. S., 20 m. E of Mayville. Its surface is undulating, and is drained by Connewango creek and other smaller streams. The soil is clay and sandy loam. Pop. in 1840, 1,144.
CHERRY-FIELD, a township of Washington co., in the state of Maine, U.S., 30 m. W of Machias, intersected by the Narragaugus river, and possessing a considerable trade. Pop. in 1840, 1,003.
CHERRY-GROVE, a village of St. Clair co., in the state of Illinois, U.S., 8 m. NE of Belleville. Its pop., which is considerable, is chiefly German.
CHERRY-HINTON, a parish of Cambridgeshire, 24 m. SE of Cambridge. Area 2,043 acres. Pop. 654.
CHERRY ISLAND. See CHERIE.
CHERRY-TREE, a township of Venango co., in the state of Pennsylvania, U.S., 230 m. WNW of Harrisburg. It is rather hilly. Pop. 598.

CHERRY-VALLEY, a township of Oswego co., in the state of New York, U.S., 55 m. W of Albany, drained by Cherry Valley creek and its branches, which flow S, and by the head streams of Canajoharie creek. The surface is hilly and the soil calcareous mould. Pop. in 1840, 3,923. The village is situated on the Great Western turnpike, 52 m. W of Albany. Pop. 1,100.—Also a township of Ashtabula co. in the state of Ohio, 202 m. NE of Columbus. It is partly level, and affords excellent pasturage. Pop. 690.
CHERRYVILLE, a village of Northampton co., in the state of Pennsylvania, 107 m. ENE of Harrisburg, consisting, in 1840, of a few dwellings.

CHERSO, an island of Austria, in the Adriatic, in the gulf of Quarnero. It is 44 m. in length from N to S; but its greatest breadth does not exceed 7 m. It has an area of about 120 sq. m. Pop. 14,000. The soil is rocky, and little corn is grown upon the island; its chief productions are wine and honey, and extensive herds of cattle are reared upon its pastures. In the interior is a lake 7 m. in circumf. -Its cap., of the same name, the ancient Crapsa, stands near the middle of the W side of the island, in N lat. 44° 57′ 4″, E long. 14° 24′ 14″. Pop. 3,600.
CHERSON. See KHERSON.

CHERT, a town of Spain, in Valencia, in the prov. and 38 m. N of Castelton-de-la-Plana, partido of San Mateo. Pop. 1,718. It is situated on a hill, which is bathed by the Arbol, and has a parish church and a custom-house. It is of considerable antiquity, and contains the ruins of a fortress and of some edifices supposed to be Roman.

CHERTSEY, a parish and market-town of Surrey, 21 m. WSW of London, on the W bank of the Thames, which is here crossed by a fine stone-bridge, 4 m. from the Southampton railway, and 21½ m. by railway from London. Area 10,020 acres. Pop. 5,347.
CHERU, a town of Persia, in the prov. of Laristan, on the coast of the Persian gulf, 40 m. W of Mount Charrack.
CHERVEIX-CUBAS, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Dordogne, cant. of Hautefort. Pop. 1,190.
CHERVES, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Vienne, cant. of Mirabeau. Pop. 1,099.

CHERVES-DE-COGNAC, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Charente, cant. and 5 m. N of Cognac. Pop. 1,424. It produces large quantities of wine and brandy. CHERVES-DE-MONTEMBŒUF, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Charente, cant. of Montembœuf, 18 m. SSW of Confolens. Pop. 1,278. It contains an iron-mine.
CHERVEUX, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Deux-Sevres, cant. of Saint-Maixent, 10 m. NE of Niort. Pop. 1,514.
CHERVILLE-STREET, a hamlet in the p. of Infra-Romsey, Southampton. Pop. 493.

CHESAPEAKE BAY, a large arm of the sea, on the E coast of N America, entering on the coast of Virginia, U.S., on the parallel of 37° N, between Cape Charles on the N, and Cape Henry on the S, which are about 12 or 15 m. apart, and stretching, in the greater part of its length, into the state of Maryland, which it divides into two sections, known as the E and the W shores. It is above 200 m. in length, and from 7 m. to 20 m. in breadth. Its area has been estimated at 3,500 sq. m. Its general depth is 9 fath. It receives the Susquehannah river at its N termination; the Potomac on the W; and James river near its month. Besides these principal streams, the Patuxent, Patapsco, Chester, Elk, Choptank, Nanticoke, and numerous other streams, flow into this common estuary, which may be regarded as receiving the drainage of above 70,000 sq. m. It is connected with Delaware bay by a canal 14 m. in length, 60 ft. wide, and 10 ft. deep, which was begun in 1823, and completed in 6 years at a cost of 2,000,000 dollars. The Newcastle and Frenchtown railroad, 164 m. in length, also extends across the peninsula between the Delaware and the C.—The Susquehannah canal, commencing at Wrightsville in Pennsylvania, opposite Columbia, and running along the W side of the Susquehannah river to Havre-de-Grace in Maryland, opens a communication between the Pennsylvania canal and the tide-water of C. bay. It is 45 m. in length, 50 ft. wide, and 5 ft. deep; and has a descent to the C. of 233 ft.--The C. and Ohio canal, now in progress of execution, is designed to connect the waters of C. bay with those of the river Ohio. The E section of this immense work was begun in 1828, and has been completed from Georgetown, in the district of Columbia, to Cumberland in Maryland, a distance of 186 m. on the N bank of the Potomac. The middle section is to leave the river at this point by the valley of Wills's creek, and crossing the Savage mountain by a tunnel 4 m. ii: length, to strike the headwaters of the Youghgany. The W section passes down the Youghgany to its confluence with the Monongahela, and thence, by the valley of the Monongahela, to Pittsburg, at the head of the steamboat navigation on the Ohio.—In 1621, Sir George Calvert. afterwards Baron Baltimore in Ireland, obtained from King James a grant of a part of Newfoundland, where he attempted to found a colony, but was foiled by the climate and local disadvantages. He then visited Virginia, and on his return obtained a grant of the territory which now constitutes Maryland. His eldest son determined to prosecute the designs of his father, and at the head of a band of emigrants entered the C. in February, 1634, and debouched at a place on the N bank of the Potomac, near its junction with the C. From this nucleus sprang all the flourishing settlements round the C. The site of Baltimore, which is the leading-port of the C., was in 1729 a farm surrounded by wild land.
CHESAUX, a town of Switzerland, in the cant. of Vaud, 10 m. N of Lausanne.—Also a town on the S side of the lake of Neuchatel, 3 m. E of Yverdun.
CHESHAM, a parish and market-town of Buckinghamshire, 29 m. WNW of London. Area 11,880 acres. Pop. 5,593.
CHESHAM-BOIS, a parish of Bucks, 11 m. NNE of Chesham. Area 810 acres. Pop. 218.

CHESHIRE, or CHESTER, a maritime county-palatine of England; bounded on the NW and N by a small part of the co. of York, the co.-palatine of Lancaster, the estuary of the Mersey, and the Irish sea; on the E by the cos. of Derby and Stafford; on the S by the co. of Salop, and a detached part of the co. of Flint; on the W by the estuary of the Dee, and the cos. of Denbigh and Flint. Its greatest length, taking it at the extreme points, from E to W, is about 50 m.; its greatest breadth, from N to S, about 30 m. Its form is oval, with a narrow neck of land, of about 6 m. in breadth, projecting on the W coast, between the estuaries of the Dee and the Mersey, nearly 20 m. into the Irish sea; and a still narrower stripe on the E, projecting about 15 m. between the cos. of York and Derby. It has been estimated to contain 1,052 sq. m., or about 673,280 statute acres, which were distributed, in 1808, nearly as follows, viz.: arable, meadow, and pasture lands, including parks and pleasure-grounds, 620,000 acres; waste lands, heaths, commons, and woods, 28,600 acres; peats, bogs, and morasses, 18,000 acres; the residue consisting of sea-sands within the estuaries of the Dee and the Mersey. The estimate of its area in the parliamentary returns for 1841 gave only 649,050 acres. A good deal of the waste land, and of the bogs and morasses, has been brought under tillage within the last forty years.—The surface of this co. being almost all a dead level, presents a landscape for the most part tame and uninteresting. A ridge of hills, but of no great altitude, runs along its E border from Lawton on the confines of Staffordshire, to its NE extremity; forming, as it were, the first steps towards the romantic mountains of Derbyshire. Another ridge extends from Malpas, on its W side, to Frodsham. It is, however, much broken. The most singular feature in it is the insulated rock of Buston, about 2 m. S of Tarporley, rising, on one side, almost perpendicularly from the plain, about 366 ft. With these exceptions, the rest of the co., comprising four-fifths of its entire extent, is remarkable for the flatness of its surface; being, probably, not more on a medium, than from 100 to 200 ft. above the level of the sea. Its appearance is that of an extended plain, thickly covered with woods, and interspersed with small lakes or meres, abounding with fish, and some of them not without beauty. Its woods, however, are nearly confined to the hedgerows, which are numerous, to the coppices that adorn gentlemen's seats, and to the parks and pleasuregrounds that surround the residences of the nobility; but, on the whole, this co. contains a great deal of valuable timber. More rain is believed to fall in this than in any other co. in England, Westmoreland and Lancashire perhaps excepted. Rivers.] The two principal rivers of C. are the Mersey and the Dee. The county is watered by numerous streams, most of them having a N course, and none of them going beyond its limits, being all conducted into the Irish sea through the estuaries of the Mersey and the Dee.—The Mersey has its remote sources in Derbyshire, and from Stockport to the Irish sea it forms the boundary between this co. and Lancashire, to which, as forming the port of Liverpool, it more properly belongs. The Dee rises in the mountainous district of Merionethshire; and approaching C. near Shocklach, forms the boundary between it and Denbighshire, till it approaches Aldford, whence, with a few windings, it flows on to Chester, the walls of which it nearly encircles. From Chester, by an artificial navigable channel, cut in 1754, at a vast expense, and which it is now proposed still further to improve, it is carried directly into the broad estuary which unites it with the Irish sea.—To these rivers the next in importance is the Weever, which has its rise in Ridley mere, on Bulkeley heath, and flowing by Nantwich to Northwich, is there joined by the Dane, a considerable stream; and a little farther on by the Peover; after which it flows by Frodsham to Winton, where it falls into the grand estuary of the Mersey. Its whole course is about 30 m., for 20 of which it has been rendered navigable, by means of various locks, for vessels of 100 tons.—The Dane, rising in Macclesfield forest, near the Threeshire mere, has at first a S course; and, for a considerable distance, forms the boundary between this county and that of Stafford. It then turns W, and flows by Congleton, and Middlewich, to Northwich, where it falls into the Weever.-The Bollin has its sources also in Macclesfield forest, and flowing in a N direction, falls into the Mersey, after a course of nearly 20 m.—The Tame rises in Yorkshire, and, flowing to the W, forms the boundary between C. and Lancashire, till it falls into the Mersey near Stockport. Canals.] Till the introduction of railroads had diminished the utility, and checked the progress of inland navigation, the C. canals afforded a constant and economical channel of commercial intercourse, and general communication, between Chester, Liverpool, and Manchester, the North of England, Staffordshire, Shropshire, &c. Few counties derived, or indeed still derive, so many advantages from the internal intercourse produced by artificial navigation, as C. The principal canals which intersect various parts of the county, are the duke of Bridgewater's, the Trent and Mersey, the Ellesmere, the Chester and Nantwich, and the Macclesfield and Peak-forest canals.—The Bridgewater canal from Manchester enters C. by crossing the Mersey, to the E of Ashton, on an aqueduct bridge. It traverses about 20 m. of this shire, passing in a SW direction near to Lymm, Thelwall, Groppenham, Daresbury, and Preston-Brook to Runcorn, where it is lowered, 95 ft., by a series of locks, into the Mersey. This was the first canal projected by Brindley, at the expense of the duke of Bridgewater, to whose magnificent undertakings the inland navigation of England, in general, is so deeply indebted. It was completed in 1766; since which time the greater part of the canals in England have been formed.—The Trent and Mersey, or Grand Trunk canal, which was commenced in 1766, proceeds SE from Preston-Brook, where the Bridgewater canal joins it, passing by Northwich, Middlewich, and Sandbach, to Church-Lawton on the S side of this county, where it enters the co. of Stafford: the whole length of this line in C. being 20 m. Near its commencement at Preston-on-the-Hill, it passes through a tunnel 1,241 yards long; at Barnton, through one of 572 yards; at Saltersfield, through one of 350 yards; and at Hermitage, through another of 130 yards.—The Ellesmere canal enters this county at Pulford, and running across the peninsula of Wirrall, effects a junction of the Dee and the Mersey. At Chester, it unites with the navigable channel of the Dee, and with the Chester and Nantwich canal; then running N by Stoke, it issues into the Mersey, thus opening a short and easy passage between Chester and Liverpool.—The Chester and Nantwich canal was completed in 1778. At Chester it forms a junction with the Dee, and with the Dee and Mersey, or Ellesmere canal; and passing Christleton, Waverton, Hargrave, and Beeston-castle, proceeds to Nantwich, where it is continued into the Birmingham and Liverpool Junction canal, which runs S, past Audlem, into Shropshire. A branch from the Chester and Nantwich canal, striking off 4 m. N of Nantwich, runs in a NE direction, to Middlewich, where it meets the Trent and Mersey canal. Between Nantwich and the point where this branch joins the Chester and Nantwich canal, on the other hand, a branch proceeds in a SW direction to the neighbourhood of Whitechurch.—The Macclesfield canal leaves the Trent and Mersey canal at Church-Lawton on the borders of the co.; runs N past Congleton and Macclesfield to Disley; and joins the Peak-forest canal, which enters the county at Whaley bridge, and passing near Disley, proceeds N by Chadkirk and Hyde-chapel, to Ashton-under-Lyne, where it crosses the Tame, and quits the co. This canal crosses the Goyt, below the junction of the Goyt and Etherow, by an aqueduct 100 ft. in height, which has three arches, each 60 ft. span, and 78 ft. high.—The Mersey and Irwell canal meets the Trent and Mersey canal at Runcorn, and running to the NE, along the border of the county, quits it near Thelwall.—In 1795, a canal to Pennington, near Leigh in Lancashire, connected the canals of Cheshire through the Bridgewater canal with the Leeds and Liverpool navigation. Railways.] The Grand Junction railway, between Birmingham and the Liverpool and Manchester railway, enters this co. near Betley, on the S, and runs through Crewe, near Sandbach, to the NE of Nantwich. It then runs a little to the W of Middlewich and Northwich, and across the valley of the Weever by a viaduct of 20 arches of 60 ft. span, and upwards of 60 ft. above the level of the valley. From this point it proceeds to the Mersey, which it crosses by a viaduct of 12 arches, under which flows the Mersey and Irwell canal as well as the Mersey itself. A railway has been executed from Birkenhead to Chester; and in connection with this railway, another between Chester and Crewe, where it meets the Grand Junction railway, and the railway from Manchester to Crewe.—The Chester and Holyhead railway, which, in its engineering works, is the most enterprising of all the English railways, begins at the terminus of the London and North-Western railway at Chester; passing under a portion of that city by a tunnel 400 yards long. After crossing the Dee, it continues its course nearly parallel with the embanked channel of that river, and along the estuary of the Dee to the town of Flint. Passing Abergale, and through a narrow valley to the S of Orme's head, it crosses the river Conway by a tubular bridge, consisting of two rectangular tubes or tunnels, each 400 ft. in length, 24 ft. high, and 15 ft. wide. It then skirts Conway-castle, and, rounding the promontory of Penmaen-Mawr, crosses the Ogmore river by a viaduct 246 yards in length, and, passing Bangor, crosses the Menal straits by another great tubular bridge. See articles Con-

WAY, and MENAI STRAITS. Soil, minerals, &c.] The soil of C. consists, for the most part, of a red, rich, clayey, or sandy loam; and, with the exception of a few limited tracts of black moor, or bogs of peat moss and heath, is eminently fertile. The under-soil is commonly clay, or marl, the plentiful use of which has materially contributed to its fertility. Salt and coal are the principal mineral productions. There are inexhaustible beds of rock-salt, and strong brine springs, near Northwich, Nantwich, Winsford, and Middlewich, along the course of the Weever and its tributaries. The salt-works at Northwich are the most famous. The salt is of two kinds, white, or transparent, and reddish brown. The former has been found by analysis to be an almost pure muriate of soda: the latter to contain a small portion of oxide of iron from which its colour is derived. The supply of salt in this co. seems quite inexhaustible; and it is more than probable that the salt manufacturers of C. could undersell the native produce of any part of the world accessible to them, if no artificial restrictions were imposed upon them. Until lately, the duty upon English salt, in the United States, varied from 100 to 400 per cent. on the prime cost; and yet the people of the United States took upwards of 4,000,000 of bushels of salt from England yearly, though a large quantity of excellent salt is manufactured within the Union. It is to be hoped that the East India company will ere long resign their monopoly of the salt trade in Bengal in favour of English commerce.—In the N districts, coal, of good quality, is found in abundance, especially in the townships of Worth and Poynton. Lead and copper are found at Alderley-edge, and the Peckforton Hills; and at the former a considerable quantity of cobalt is obtained, from which smalt is manufactured scarcely to be distinguished from that of Saxony.—The red grit rock is the most prevalent stone of the county, and of this, most of the towns and villages are built. The quarries at Runcorn, Manley, and Great- Bebington, are the most important. Agriculture.] Dairy produce in cheese and butter is the principal object of attention with the C. husbandman. Dr. Holland estimated the number of cows kept in this county, for dairy purposes, in 1808, at 92,000. Supposing each cow to yield, at an average, 21 cwt. of cheese, the whole annual produce, even at that period, would be 11,500 tons. In some parts of the co. potatoes are extensively cultivated. Oats, wheat, and barley, are also grown; but arable husbandry, being deemed of inferior importance, is but indifferently understood; while sheep-husbandry is here scarcely known. There are many parcels of land in this co., averaging 10 acres each, which have been appropriated as small farms; but the greater portion of the county is retained and cultivated by gentlemen who reside on their own estates, which are generally large. On the whole, C. is inferior to many other counties in its agriculture; while it contains more means of improved husbandry than most. It wants only the application of capital, and the superintendence of men of true practical science, to become one of the finest agricultural counties in England. Trade, manufactures, &c.] The principal trade of this county consists in cheese, potatoes, and salt. In addition to the great home-consumption of cheese, immense quantities are sold not only throughout England, but also throughout Europe. Potatoes are sent in great quantities to Liverpool, Manchester, and other markets in Lancashire. The extensive trade carried on in salt, and the vast revenue derived from its duty, at one time rendered it an object of even national importance; and it is still one of the most important staple commodities of the county. About 60,000 tons of salt are annually taken from the pits of Northwich alone; and not less than 45,000 tons are manufactured at the same place from brine-springs. In 1831, nearly 300 men were employed in Eddisbury and Northwich hundreds in manufacturing salt. Lead, calamine, cast-iron, copper-plates, and brass, are largely exported.—The most flourishing manufacture is that of cotton, which has extended into this co. from Lancashire, and is extensively carried on in and around Stockport, Macclesfield, Marple, Congleton, and Sandbach. Muslin and calico, thread and silk-buttons, are manufactured at Macclesfield, where there are also thriving silk-mills. There are also silk-mills at Congleton and Stockport. Macclesfield hundred is the principal manufacturing district. In 1831 more than 6,000 men were employed in manufacturing cotton and calico in this hundred; nearly 1,000 in the manufacture of silk, and about 5,000 in manufacturing cotton and silk promiscuously. The manufacture of hats for exportation at Stockport, of shoes at Sandbach and Nantwich, of woollen-cloths in the same district, and of gloves at Chester, is extensive. Tanning is carried on throughout the whole county, but more particularly in the middle and northern parts, where there are extensive plantations and spontaneous growths of oak, which furnish abundance of tanner's bark. The copper, lead, and iron of the Alderly-edge, and Peckforton hills, are also manufactured in the co., and constitute a small item in the export trade. Statistics, &c.] The pop. of this county in 1800 was 191,751; in 1811, 227,031, showing an increase of 18 per cent. In 1821, it was 270,098, being an increase of 19 per cent. in 10 years; in 1831, 334,391, being an increase of 24 per cent.; in 1841, 395,660, being an increase of 18.3 per cent. The average increase over all England betwixt the years 1831 and 1841 was 14.5 per cent. Of the total pop. in 1841, 300,170 were natives of the co.; 79,012 were born in other cos. of England or Wales; 1,808 were natives of Scotland; 11,577 of Ireland; and 385 were foreigners, or born in foreign parts.-The amount of poor-rate assessment, on an average of three years, in 1748-50, was £14,946; in 1815, it was £142,409; in 1828, £139,412; in 1847, £80,332. The proportion per cent. of paupers returned on the estimated pop., in 1847, [439,160,] was 5.5 per cent.; that for all England being 8.8 per cent.—The expenditure per head for relief to the poor, on the estimated pop. in 1847, was 3s. 8d.; that for all England being 6s. 14d. -The amount of real property assessed to the property and income tax, in 1815, was £1,083,083; in The principal market-towns are Altringham, Congleton, Frodsham, Knutsford, Macclesfield, Malpas, Middlewich, Nantwich, Northwich, Sandbach, Stockport, and Tarporley.—The county returns 4 members to parliament: 2 for the northern, and 2 for the southern divisions. The northern division consists of the hundreds of Macclesfield and Bucklow; the southern comprises the other hundreds. The total number of electors for the northern division, in 1842-3, was 6,425; for the southern, 8,136. Besides these, 2 members are returned for each of the boroughs of Chester, Macclesfield, and Stockport. History.] Under the Britons, Cheshire, or Chestershire, formed part of the territory of the Cornavii. In the first Roman division of the country it was included in Britannia-Superior; under the second, in Flavia Casariensis. After the Conquest, it was bestowed by the Conqueror upon his nephew, Hugh de Avaranches. In the reign of Henry III., John, earl of Chester, dying without male issue, that monarch seized upon the earldom, which he bestowed upon his eldest son, Prince Edward. It was erected into a principality by Richard II. Under his successor, it again became a co.-palatine, and continued under the king's eldest sons, as earls of Chester, to be governed by a separate and an independent jurisdiction. Under Henry the city of Chester was separated from the co., being constituted a co. of itself. Under Henry VIII., the privileges of the counties-palatine were greatly curtailed, and, in return, two knights were allowed in parliament to represent the county, and two burgesses the city of Chester. Acts passed in 1830 terminated the separate jurisdiction of the co.-palatine. CHE

CHESHIRE, a co. in the state of New Hampshire, U.S., comprising an area of 442 sq. m., watered by the Ashuelot river and its branches, and bordered on the W by the Connecticut river. In the SE is Grand Monadnock mountain, 3,000 ft. above sea-level, and in the township of Walpole is the celebrated cataract of Bellows falls. Pop. in 1830, 27,016; in 1840, 26,429. Cap., Keene.—Also a township in Berkshire co., in Massachusetts, 130 m. W by N of Boston. Pop. 985.—Also a township in New Haven co., in Connecticut, 25 m. SSW of Hartford on the Quennepiac river. Pop. 1,529.—Also a v. in Canandaigna township, in Ontario co., in the state of New York, 203 m. W by N of Albany.—Also a township in Gallia co., in Ohio, 102 m. SSE of Columbus, on the Ohio river. Pop. 791.
CHESHUNT, a parish of Hertfordshire, 41 m. SSW of Hoddesdon, on the New River and near the London and Cambridge railway. Area 8,430 acres. Pop. 5,402.
CHESIL BANK, a bank in the British channel, between the coast of Dorset and the island of Portland, stretching SE from Abbotsbury. It is 9 m. in length, and consists of sand and gravel.
CHESILBORNE, a parish of Dorset, 10 m. WSW of Blandford Forum. Area 1,850 acres Pop. 346.
CHESLYN-HAY, an extra parochial district of Staffordshire. Pop. 774.

CHESME, or TCHISME, a small town of Turkey in Asia, in the sanjak of Siglah, 35 m. W by S of Smyrna, at the bottom of a small bay about 2 m. in length, and 1 m. broad. On the 7th of July, 1770, the Russian fleet under Count Orloff having defeated the Turkish fleet a little above the N entrance of this bay, the fugitive vessels took refuge in this bay, where they were totally destroyed by fire-ships. CHESMEH-ALI [i. e. 'the Fountain of Ali], a hamlet of Persia, in the NW of Khorasan, on the flows from this tank is joined by several rivulets, and soon becomes a moderately sized stream which, flowing SE, passes Damghan. Vaud, on an affluent of the Arve, 2 m. ESE of Geneva.

CHESNUT HILL, a village of Germantown township, Philadelphia co., in the state of Pennsylvania, U.S., 9 m. W of Philadelphia. Pop. in 1840, 600.— Also a township of Monroe co., partly level and watered by Head's creek. Pop. 1,318.
CHESNUT RIDGE, a branch of the Alleghany mountains, U. S., extending NE from the state of Maryland through Fayette and Westmoreland counties, in the state of Pennsylvania.

CHESSO, a village of Bamburra, in Western Africa, between Oulasso and Pala, in about 11° 40' N lat. It is formed of several little enclosures very near to each other. The surrounding country is bare, and level, and stony. The vegetation is chiefly bombaces and baobabs.—Caille.

CHESSY, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Rhône, cant. of Bois-d'Oingt, on the r. bank of the Azergue, 12 m. SSW of Villefranche. Pop. in 1841, 762. In the locality are extensive copper-mines.

CHEST, a township of Clearfield co., in the state of Pennsylvania, U.S., 150 m. NW of Harrisburg. It presents a hilly surface, and is watered by the W branch of the Susquehannah river. Its soil is loamy.

CHESTAKOV, a town of Russia in Europe, in the gov. and 25 m. NNE of Viatka, district and 15 m. N of Slobodskoï, on the r. bank of the Viatka. Pop. 1,200. In the environs are the ruins of an ancient town and several iron-mines.
CHESTE, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 19 m. W of Valencia, partido of Enguera. Pop. 1,740. It contains a church and a custom-house, and has an extensive entrepot trade.

CHESTER, a city and county of itself, locally situated in the co.-palatine of Chester, of which it is the capital; 179 m. by railway NW of London, and 21 m. S by E of Liverpool. Its situation is very picturesque, on the N bank of the Dee, which bounds it on the S and W, and 6 m. above the estuary of that river. It stands on a rocky elevation, half-encircled by a bend of the noble river; and has to the eye of a stranger a striking and picturesque appearance. It is walled all around, the walls being nearly 2 m. in circumf.; and so broad as to admit, even where narrowest, of two persons walking abreast. They form indeed a fine promenade, and afford delightful views of the estuary of the Dee, the neighbouring county of Flint, and the distant mountains of Wales. The town is composed of four principal streets runuing from a common centre—called here the Pentise, elsewhere it would be called the cross-to the four cardinal points of the compass, each terminated by an arched gateway, named from their accidents, Bridge-gate, Water-gate, East-gate, and North-gate. The keeping of these gates was in former times an office of great importance and high honour; and as such, claimed by the first families of the kingdom. The streets are cut down several feet into the solid rock, and level with them are low shops or warehouses, above which are balustrated galleries,-or rows, as they are called by the inhabitants, -which serve the purposes of side-pavements for foot-passengers. Along these rows are shops and houses, the upper stories being built over them, so that they seem to run along the first floors of the houses. These rows are connected with the streets by flights of steps at convenient distances. In the old buildings they are closed in with heavy wooden railings and immense pillars of oak, with transverse beams supporting the upper stories; in the more modern buildings, they have been greatly improved, and their appearance rendered light and airy by the use of elegantly formed iron railings. The streets, though brilliantly lighted with gas, are indifferently paved. The new bridge across the Dee is particularly worthy of notice. It consists of only one arch, but one of the largest ever attempted with stone, being 200 ft. in span. It is built of Peckforton stone, with granite groins; and cost £40,000.—The Linen-hall in Watergate street is a splendid pile of building. The Exchange, in Northgate street, containing concert-rooms and courts of justice, is a handsome building ornamented with columns. Union-hall and Commercialhall were built by subscription in 1809 and 1815, to accommodate the merchants from Manchester and Yorkshire at the fairs of July and August. In the latter of these halls are held the quarter-sessions and meetings for electing the city officers. The city-gaol and house-of-correction are surrounded by a brick wall of an oblong form, slightly cut off at the angles. They consist of two stories, comprising 25 cells, 10 wards, 10 airing-yards, and 18 rooms.—Of all the buildings in the city, however, that called the castle is decidedly the most splendid. It stands on the site of the old castle, nearly the whole of which has been taken down, and constitutes a general and elegant design for county-courts, gaols, barracks, armoury, and other edifices. The county-gaol, a magnificent structure, scarcely exceeded by any in the kingdom, forms a prominent portion of this general design. As originally constructed, it consisted of two separate buildings, one forming three sides of a square, containing apartments for debtors; the other, of a semicircular shape, for criminal prisoners, with five divisions, and the same number of yards. The number of separate cells amounts to 56, independently of two detached ones used for condemned prisoners. A considerable addition has been made to the prison by the enclosure of an extensive area, and the erection of a new building containing 54 cells and other apartments.-On the east side of the yard are the range of barracks; and on the opposite side is the armoury, large enough, it is said, to store 30,000 stand of arms. The castle is a royal fortress, with a governor, lieutenant-governor, &c .- This city is the seat of a diocese, comprehending the co. of Chester, and the SW portion of Lancaster, with the exception of the p. of Leigh. The gross yearly income of the see amounts to £3,951. The rectory of Waverton is permanently annexed to the bishopric, and a prebend in Durham cathedral is held in commendam. The gross yearly income of the dean and chapter is £2,135. The corporation consists of the dean and six prebendaries. The cathedral is situated on the E side of North-gate street. It is a spacious but irregular pile, formed of the red stone of the co.; and, with the exception of a few fragments, appears to have been built in the reigns of Henry VI., VII., and VIII. The tower, which is 127 ft. high, springs from four pillars. The W front is deemed very handsome. The ancient chapter-house, in the E walk of the cloister, is a noble hall, of grand dimensions, erected by Randolph, first Earl of Chester. Besides the castle and the liberties of the cathedral, which are extra-parochial, 11 parishes are within the city. There are also places of worship for Independents, Baptists, and Wesleyan, Primitive, and Calvinistic Methodists. The charitable institutions of the city are very numerous.—The pop. of C. in 1801 was 14,977; in 1841, 23,115. The area of the p. was 3,010 acres; and the number of inhabited houses in 1841, 4,647.—On the abridgment of its privileges as a co.-palatine, C. was allowed to send 2 members to parliament, a privilege which it still enjoys under the reform act. The boundaries of the new borough are the same with those of the old, except that the new includes a portion of the township of Great- Boughton. The number of electors registered in 1837 was 2,298; in 1847, 2,669. The pop. of the parl. borough in 1831 amounted to 20,088; in 1841, to 22,961. This city has had a commission of the peace granted, and a court of quarter-sessions. The port of C. has bonding warehouses for wine and spirits, wood-goods, and corn. It was at one time a place of great trade; but the gradual accumulation of sand in the mouth of the river rendered it impossible for vessels of any burden to enter it, and as a port it was nearly deserted so early as the middle of the 15th cent. By the river Dee navigation company, incorporated about the beginning of the 18th cent., a new channel was cut for the river, whereby between 2,000 and 3,000 acres of land were redeemed from the sea, and the navigation so much improved that vessels of 600 tons burden can come up to the port; yet its commerce has not been restored, being, with the exception of a few ships which visit Spain, Portugal, the Mediterranean, and the Baltic, chiefly confined to Ireland. Formerly a considerable trade was carried on in Irish linens; a linen company was established, and a large hall or market-place built for their business; this trade has, however, disappeared, and the hall is now used for the cheese-fairs which take place here eight times in the year. The imports are butter, provisions, hides, tallow, timber, iron, hemp, flax, feathers, lamb and kid skins, fruit, oil, wine, barilla, and cork, from Spain and Portugal. The exports are cheese, lead, calamine, copper-plates, cast-iron, and coals. The manufactures of C. are gloves, tobacco, tobacco-pipes said to be of a very superior quality, snuff, white-lead, shot, leather, &c. For an account of the various railroads and canals which connect the city of C. with the co.palatine and surrounding cos., see Cheshire. Shipbuilding is carried on here to a very considerable extent, ten or twelve ships being sometimes on the stocks at one time. Races are held here in the beginning of May, and are attended by an immense concourse of people. They are run upon a fine level called Roodeye, belonging to the corporation, immediately beneath the walls, from which they can be seen to great advantage. History ] C. is unquestionably a place of great antiquity. It was noticed by Roman geographers, under the names of Deunana and Deva. In nothing is the evidence of the Roman possession of Great Britain more manifest than in the frequent occurrence of the name Chester, the Latin castrum or castra It is found in Manchester, Dorchester, Grantchester, &c. Sometimes it assumes the form of Caster, as Doncaster, Tadcaster. Sometimes it becomes much contracted, as in Exeter, Wroxeter. It occurs here without any prefix; but anciently this city was frequently termed West Chester. At the Conquest, Cheshire having been granted to Hugh Lupus, with the Saxon title of Earl, but with privileges which seldom accompanied such concessions, the shire became a co.-palatine with peculiar courts, and charged with the custody of its own records; and Lupus lived in C. castle, where he held his courts and parliaments. The succeeding six earls of this family maintained the same state; but the line of succession terminated in the reign of Henry III., and from that time the earldom of C. has been assumed by the Crown, or by other members of the royal house. During the contest between Henry and his barons, C. was held by the earl of Derby for that monarch. In the contest with Charles I., the city suffered all the misery of a protracted siege; and from its towers the king had the mortification to witness the total route of his forces, under Sir Marmaduke Langdale, on Rowston moor, by the Parliamentary troops, under Poyntz and Jones.

CHESTER, a county of the state of Pennsylvania, U. S., comprising an area of 792 sq. m., drained by Brandywine and French creeks, a branch of Schuylkill and the head waters of Elk rivers, and intersected by the Columbia and Philadelphia railroad. It is hilly in the W, and the soil is diversified, but it is generally well-cultivated. Pop. in 1830, 50,910; in 1840, 57,515. Its cap. is Westchester.—Also a district of South Carolina, comprising an agreeably diversified area of 600 sq. m., bounded by Catawba river on the E, and on the W by Broad river. Pop. in 1830, 17,182; in 1840, 17,747, of whom 9,889 were whites, 7,722 slaves, and 136 free coloured. Its cap. is Chesterville.—Also a township of Rockingham co., in the state of New Hampshire, 22 m. SSE of Concord. It has a hilly surface, and is watered by a branch of Exeter river. In the NW is Massabesick pond, containing a superficies of 1,500 acres. The soil is generally fertile. Pop. 2,173. The village is finely situated, and has a considerable trade.—Also a township of Penobscot co., in the state of Maine. Pop. 277.—Also a township of Windsor co., in the state of Vermont, 83 m. S of Montpelier. Its surface is undulating, and its soil fertile. Pop. 2,305. It contains 2 flourishing villages.—Also a township of Eaton co., in the state of Michigan. Pop. 195.—Also a township of Hampden co., in the state of Massachusetts, 116 m. SW of Boston. It is generally mountainous, and is drained by branches of Westfield river. It affords excellent pasturage. Pop. 1,632.—Also a township of Middlesex co., in the state of Connecticut, 31 m. SE of Hartford. Pop. 974. - Also a village of Goshen township, Orange co., in the state of New York, 102 m. SW of Albany, on the line of the New York and Erie railway, and consisting in 1840 of about 30 dwellings.-Also a township of Warren co, in the same state, 18 m. NW of Caldwell, watered by Hudson river and its tributaries. It has a mountainous surface; but in the valleys its soil, consisting of sandy loam, is highly productive. Pop. 1,633.- CHE Also a township of Morris co., in the state of New Jersey, 50 m. NE of Trenton, drained by Black river and the tributaries of the W branch of Raritan river. It possesses an undulating surface. The soil, which is loamy, is well-cultivated. Pop. 1,321. The village, consisting of about 30 dwellings, stretches about a m. along the base of an isolated mountain.— Also a village, cap. of Delaware co., in the state of Pennsylvania, 14 m. SW of Philadelphia, on the W bank of the Delaware river. It was originally named Upland, and is the oldest township in the state. Pop. 1,790.—Also a township of Wayne co., in the same state, on the W bank of the Delaware river. Pop. 1,993.—Also a village of Randolph co., in the state of Illinois, 149 m. SE of Springfield. It occupies an elevated tract, on the E bank of the Mississippi, 2 m. below the confluence of the Kaskasia river, and is an important commercial depot. Pop. about 400.—Also a township of Wabash co., in the state of Indiana. Pop. 536.—Also a township of Meigs co., in the state of Ohio, 95 m. SSE of Columbus. Pop. 1,497. The village is on the N side of Shade river.—Also a township of Burlington co., in the state of New Jersey, 9 m. SW of Mount Holly, drained by Rancocus, Pensauken, and Pompeston creeks, and Swede's branch. It is level, and is well cultivated. The soil consists of sandy loam. Pop. 2,603.—Also a township of Knox co., in the state of Ohio, 50 m. NNE of Columbus, watered by a branch of Vernon river. Pop. 1,295.—Also a township of Butler co., in the same state. Pop. 190.—Also a township of Clinton co., in the same state, watered by tributaries of Little Miami river, and possessing a fertile and well-cultivated soil. Pop. 1,784.—Also a township of Geauga co., in the same state, 160 m. NE of Columbus. It is hilly, but affords good pasturage. Pop. 962.

CHESTER, a township of Nova Scotia, in the county of Lunenburg, on Mahone bay. It is covered to a great extent with spruce and fir timber, is well-watered, and capable of cultivation. It affords lime, yellow-ochre, and pipe-clay; and indications of coal have been discovered near Chester-town. This town lies on the N side of the bay, about 9 m. from its mouth; and is extremely flourishing, carrying on an active trade in timber and fish. The bay is capable of affording safe and commodious anchorage to vessels of the largest dimensions.
CHESTER (LITTLE), a township in the p. of St. Alkmund, Derbyshire. Pop. in 1801, 181; in 1841, 364.

CHESTER (West), a settlement of Nova Scotia, in Cumberland co., situated on the summit of the Cobequid highlands. It possesses a good soil, but from the disadvantages of its position is declining in importance.
CHESTERBLADE, a chapelry in the p. of Evercreek, Somerset, 31 m. ESE of Shepton - Mallet. Pop. 57.

CHESTERFIELD, a parish and market-town of Derbyshire, 24 m. NNE of Derby, on the side of a hill, between the Hipper and Rother.—The parish includes the hamlets of Calow, Newbold and Dunstan, and Pilsley, the townships of Hasland, Temple Normanton, Tapton, and Walton, and the chapelry of Brimington. Area 13,160 acres. Pop. in 1801, 7,593; in 1831, 10,688; in 1841, 11,281. The town is large, and irregularly built. The church is cruciform; and is remarkable for its spire, which rises to the height of 230 ft., and, like the leaning tower of Pisa, is so singularly twisted and distorted that it seems to lean in whatever direction it is approached. It has a considerable trade, and extensive manufactories of lace, cotton, and silk, with tanneries and fellmongers' works; but it is considered as more par- リングラント リング ストラント トリング アクト アクリン アイドン アイン アン・コン・コン アルス ストリー カーカー カーカー カーカー カーカー カーカー ticularly belonging to the hosiery and lace districts, a large portion of the population being occupied in these manufactures. The lace-trade has been considerably extended within the last few years.-The neighbourhood abounds with coal, iron-stone, and lead-ore, all of which are successfully wrought. There are several iron-foundries in the vicinity of the town; and there are potteries employing some hundreds of people. The sessions for the hundred, and once a-year for the county, are held in the town-hall. Races are held annually in autumn.—The Chesterfield canal, which commences in the tideway of the Trent, and is 46 m. in length, terminates at C. The North Midland railway between Derby and Leeds crosses this canal near Brimington, and passes C. at a point 241 m. from Derby, and 153 m. from Masbro'. At Rotherham this railway is connected with Sheffield by the Sheffield and Rotherham railway.

CHESTERFIELD, a county of the state of Virginia, U. S., comprising a superficies, somewhat broken, of 456 sq. m., bounded on the N and NE by James river, and on the S and SW by the Appomattox river. Pop. in 1830, 11,689; in 1840, 17,148, of whom 7,859 were whites, 8,702 slaves, and 587 free coloured. The cap., of the same name, consisting of a few dwellings, is 16 m. SW of Richmond.— Also a district of South Carolina, comprising an area of 750 sq. m., bounded on the NE by Great Pedee river, by Lynch's creek on the SW, and intersected by Black creek. Excepting on the banks of the streams, the soil is poor and sandy. Pop. in 1830, 8,472; in 1840, 8,574, of whom 5,537 were whites, 2,871 slaves, and 166 free coloured. The cap., of the same name, is situated on Thompson's creek, an affluent of Great Pedee river, 115 m. NNE of Columbia.—Also a township of Cheshire co., in the state of New Hampshire, 59 m. SW of Concord, on Connecticut river, opposite Brattleboro, in the state of Vermont; and watered also by Cat's Bane and Partridge brooks, and Spafford's lake, a beautiful sheet of water, covering an area of 526 acres. The surface is diversified, and well adapted to the culture of Indian corn and to pasturage. Pop. in 1840, 1,765.—Also a township of Hampshire co., in the state of Massachusetts, 11 m. WNW of Northampton, drained by a branch of Westfield river, which affords good water-power. The surface is hilly, but affords excellent pasturage, and is adapted to the culture of Indian corn. Pop. 1,132.—Also a village of Montville township, New London co., in the state of Connecticut, 36 m. SE of Hartford .- Also a township of Lucas co., in the state of Ohio, 170 m. NNW of Columbus, watered by Bean creek, an affluent of Maumee river. Pop. 301.—Also a township of Burlington co., in the state of New Jersey, 12 m. NE of Mount Holly, bounded on the W by the Delaware river, and intersected by its tributaries Crosswicks and Black creeks. Its surface is level, and its soil consists of sand and clayey loam. Pop. 3,438.—Also a township of Essex co., in the state of New York, 150 m. N of Albany, bordered on the E by Lake Champlain, and intersected by Ausable river. It has landing-places at Ports Douglas and Kent, and at Keeseville. The surface is mountainous, and the soil sand and clayey loam. Pop. 2,716.

CHESTERFIELD INLET, a gulf of British North America, in Churchill co., extending 300 m. WNW from the NW side of Hudson's bay. It is about 15 m. in average breadth, and is thickly studded with islands of different dimensions. The upper part, named Bakers lake, receives the waters of several extensive lakes conveyed by the Doobaunt and Cathawchaga rivers.

CHESTERFORD (GREAT), a parish of Essex 4 CHE m. NNW of Saffron-Walden, and intersected by the London and Cambridge railway. Area 3,030 acres. Pop. 917.—The village of C. is pleasantly situated on the E bank of the Granta.
CHESTERFORD (LITTLE), a parish of Essex, 3 m. NW of Saffron - Walden. Area 1,260 acres. Pop. 229.

CHESTER-LE-STREET, a parish and township of the co. of Durham. Area of p. 31,260 acres. Pop. 16,359. The township is 6 m. N of Durham, in a valley W of the Wear, intersected by the Great North of England railway, and on the Roman military road between Durham and Newcastle. It consists of a single street about 1 m. in length. In the vicinity is a productive salt spring. Area 2,940 acres. Pop. 2,599.
CHESTERNIA, a town of Russia in Europe, in the gov., district, and 75 m. NNE of Kherson, on the l. bank of the Ingouletz. Pop. 1,200.
CHESTER SPRINGS, a village of Pikeland township, Chester co., in the state of Pennsylvania, U. S., 72 m. SE of Harrisburg, resorted to as a watering place.

CHESTERTON, a parish of Cambridgeshire, about a m. N of Cambridge, on the Cam. Area 3,080 acres. Pop. 1,617.—Also a parish of Huntingdonshire, 5 m. NNW of Stilton, bounded on the S by the Nen. Area 1,440 acres. Pop. 129.—Also a parish of Oxfordshire, 2 m. SW of Bicester, on a branch of the Ray. Area 2,850 acres. Pop. 393.— Also a township of Staffordshire, 2 m. NNW of Newcastle-under-Lyne, near the Manchester and Birmingham railway. Pop. 1,207.—Also a parish of Warwickshire, 54 m. NNE of Kington, on a branch of the Avon. Area 2,960 acres. Pop. 192.

CHESTERTOWN, a village of Chester township, Warren co., in the state of New York, U. S., 81 m. N of Albany. Pop. in 1840, about 350.—Also a seaport and village, cap. of Kent co., in the state of Maryland, 54 m. NE of Annapolis, on the W side of Chester river, which is here crossed by a bridge, 30 m. above its entrance into Chesapeake Bay. Pop. in 1840, nearly 1,000.

CHESTERVILLE, a township of Franklin co., in the state of Maine, 26 m. NW of Augusta, watered by Wilson's stream, a branch of Sandy river. Pop. in 1840, 1,098.—Also a village of Chester township, Knox co., in the state of Ohio, 53 m. NNE of Columbus. Pop. 283.
CHESUNCOOK, a lake of the state of Maine, U. S., 15 m. NE of Moosehead lake. It is traversed by the Penobscot river.
CHESWARDINE, a parish and township of Salop, 4½ m. SSE of Drayton, near the Birmingham and Liverpool canal. Area of p. 6,070 acres. Pop. 1,015. Pop. of township, 367.
CHESWICK, a township of the co. of Durham,— locally in Northumberland,—10 m. NNW of Belford. Pop. 290.
